links.rationale: Asks students to describe how individuals have changed communities past and present, which is the concept's claim that individual actions shaped the world people live in now; cross-grade (K-2 concept, TX G3), and only the 'individuals' strand of this bundled standard attaches.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify named historical figures who helped shape communities, exactly the concept's test of studying specific figures' contributions; cross-grade (K-2 concept, TX G3).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to describe how named individuals contributed to the expansion and creation of communities, satisfying the concept's test that individual actions shaped the world people live in now; cross-grade (K-2 concept, TX G3).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks for the historical influence of individuals on contemporary societies, which is the concept's test that individual actions shaped the world people live in now; cross-grade (concept band K-2).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to describe the contributions of individuals from past and present societies, the same ask as the concept's existing 'contributions made by these figures' member; cross-grade (concept band K-2).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks for the roles significant individuals played in a historical event, which touches the concept's contributions-of-figures test but is anchored to the Revolution rather than to shaping the present; cross-grade (concept band K-2).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to describe the contributions of significant historical figures, the same ask as the concept's existing contributions-of-figures member; cross-grade (concept band K-2).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain how work provides income, which is precisely the concept's test that people earn income by working. Note the concept has an unresolved duplicate (c-people-earn-income-by-working-2); attached to the primary only.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain how climate and natural resources influenced the location of economic activities, which is the concept's test that physical geography shapes the work people do.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify and explain how geographic factors have influenced the location of economic activities, directly the concept's test.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the geographic factors responsible for where economic activities are located, i.e. how physical geography shapes the work people do; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to analyze how physical characteristics of the environment influenced economic activities and settlement, which is the membership test; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify the significance of various ethnic and cultural celebrations, which is the concept's test that communities contain distinct cultural groups known by their customs and celebrations.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to compare ethnic and cultural celebrations, which requires recognizing multiple distinct cultural groups and the celebrations that distinguish them.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the significance of ethnic and cultural celebrations across communities, which is the concept's test that communities contain diverse cultural groups distinguished by their celebrations.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to compare ethnic and cultural celebrations between the local community and others, directly satisfying the concept's test.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify the customs, celebrations, and traditions of various cultural, regional, and local groups, which is exactly the concept's test; the content is generic cultural diversity despite sitting in TX's Texas-history year.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to describe the customs and traditions of various racial, ethnic, and religious groups, satisfying the concept's test that communities contain diverse cultural groups with distinguishing customs.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to define a multicultural society, i.e. to recognize that a society contains diverse cultural groups; the customs-and-celebrations clause is absent; cross-grade (concept band K-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain how a society's diversity shows up in its cultural activities and celebrations, which is the concept's test including its customs-and-celebrations clause; cross-grade (concept band K-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Lists the duties of good citizenship including participation in government and voting, which is the duties half of the concept's test; the standard never addresses the rights citizens hold.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify and describe individual acts of civic responsibility — obeying laws, jury service, voting — which is the concept's test of the duties citizens owe as members of a community.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Explains how individuals participate voluntarily in civic affairs, which enumerates civic duties but frames them as optional participation rather than duties owed, and omits rights entirely.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Explicitly asks students to explain the duty of the individual in elections, which is the duties clause of the concept's test; cross-grade content-wise this is generic civics inside TX's Texas-history year.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain why individuals have a duty to participate in civic affairs, which is the concept's test of the duties citizens owe in return for membership; rights are not addressed.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to describe the roles and responsibilities of citizens across societies; satisfies the duties half of the test but not the rights half; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the duty of civic participation, which is a duty citizens owe as members of a nation; the rights half of the test is absent; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the relationships among rights, responsibilities, and duties, which is precisely the rights-and-duties-in-return test; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the rights citizens hold; satisfies the rights half of the test, and matches the shape of a state-constitution civics standard. The duties half is carried by 7.15B.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain citizens' civic responsibilities and the importance of civic participation, i.e. the duties owed; the rights half is carried by 7.15A.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify examples of responsible citizenship (obeying laws, staying informed, voting, jury service), which is the duties-owed half of the test stated directly; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify functions of government such as establishing order, providing security, and managing conflict — the order-and-security purposes the concept enumerates, though the standard frames them as functions of government rather than of laws.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify governmental services in the community and explain their value, which is the 'public services' purpose the concept enumerates; framed as government services rather than as what laws accomplish.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Defines and exemplifies scarcity, the limited-resources half of the concept's test, but never reaches the forced choice or the alternative given up; attached to the K-5 duplicate only, not its merge twin.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to distinguish between producing and consuming, which is the concept's test of distinguishing the producer role from the consumer role.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify ways in which people are both producers and consumers, which is the concept's explicit 'one person can occupy both roles' clause.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to analyze the taxation following the French and Indian War and colonist responses such as the Boston Tea Party, which is the concept's test of tracing the taxes and protests that turned grievances into a movement against imperial rule.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Traces the taxes, acts, and incidents (Stamp Act, Intolerable Acts, lack of representation, post-French-and-Indian-War policy) that turned colonial grievances into revolt; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to summarize the results of a colonial war for independence including the founding of the new nation, matching the concept's outcome framing but not the description's clause about major battles or why one side prevailed.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Names the major battles of a colonial war for independence and its concluding treaty, matching the concept's battles-and-outcome test; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify the purposes of the Declaration of Independence, which is the 'purpose' clause of the concept's test, though the standard bundles the Constitution and Bill of Rights alongside it.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: 'Consent of the governed' is a founding ideal of a declaration of independence and sits under a stem about ideas in historical documents, but the standard does not tie the ideal to the document itself.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify the intent, meaning, and importance of the Declaration of Independence, which is the concept's purpose-and-ideals test; the standard bundles two other documents alongside it. Cross-grade context: TX G4 is Texas history but this element is US founding content.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the purposes, key elements, and importance of the Declaration of Independence, which is squarely the concept's membership test.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Enumerates freedom of religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition and asks students to describe them, satisfying the concept's test of identifying First Amendment freedoms; the standard also carries non-First-Amendment rights that have no home here.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the importance of free speech and press, which are First Amendment freedoms, in a democratic society; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to describe the importance of free speech and press, First Amendment freedoms, in a constitutional republic; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to analyze the impact of the First Amendment's religious-freedom guarantee on American life, i.e. the importance of a First Amendment freedom; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks for the purpose of the Bill of Rights, which gestures at why such limits were written into a constitution, but the standard never states that the document constrains government.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks for the intent and importance of the Bill of Rights, approaching the concept's 'why such limits were written' clause, but never states that the document constrains government power.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the reasons for the creation of the Bill of Rights and its importance, which is the concept's test of why limits on government were written into a constitution.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain how colonial grievances were addressed in the Constitution and the Bill of Rights, i.e. why such limits were written into a constitution; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to summarize the rights enumerated in a bill of rights; satisfies the enumeration half of the test, less so the explicit constrains-government half; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain how Industrial Revolution changes led to conflict among sections, which is the concept's test that differing regional economies produced conflicting interests between sections of a nation. (Concept is flagged needsReview as over-broad.)

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain how the expansion of slavery — a regional institution — caused sectionalism and states'-rights conflict, which is the concept's test of institutions producing conflicting sectional interests.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the choices people can make about earning, spending, and saving money, which touches the concept's saving-versus-spending test but does not ask why saving affects financial well-being or name the parts of a budget; cross-grade (concept is 3-5, standard is grade 2).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Identifies ways of earning, spending, saving, and donating money, which names the components of personal finance but stops short of the concept's test of laying out a budget or explaining why saving over spending matters.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to create a budget allocating money for spending and saving, which is directly the concept's test of identifying the parts of a personal budget.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to compare the ways of life of distinct Indigenous groups, which is the comparison the concept tests, but the standard never states that those ways of life arose from the environments the groups lived in.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Describes the cultural regions Indigenous peoples lived in, supplying the concept's region-to-culture link but not the ways of life the concept asks students to derive from those environments.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to compare Indigenous cultures grouped by region (Gulf, Plains, Puebloan, Southeastern), which is the concept's compare-Indigenous-ways-of-life-across-regions test; the environment-as-cause clause is implied, not stated. Cross-grade (concept band 3-5) and a rare case where Texas-history content generalizes.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: The immigration-and-migration strand asks for their effects on economic development and growth of a region, which is the concept's test, but the standard bundles exploration and limited resources, which have no home here.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to analyze the effects of immigration and migration on economic development and growth, which is the concept's test that movement of people into a region changes conditions there; cross-grade band (3-8 concept, TX G5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: States exactly the membership test: human migration into a place changes the character of that place and region.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain how immigration and migration into a region changed that region, which is the membership test.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Changing population distribution producing new needs for services is movement of people changing conditions in a region, but the standard is framed around growth and demography rather than migration.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: States the membership test almost verbatim: why immigrant groups came and where they settled. Cross-grade (concept band 3-5); the state setting is incidental to the idea.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: States the membership test almost verbatim: which groups settled and why they immigrated; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Names the four-resource taxonomy verbatim (natural resources, labor, capital, entrepreneurs) and asks how they shape an economy's production; cross-grade (concept band 3-5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to describe the impact of mass production, specialization, and division of labor on economic growth, which is the concept's test that dividing work into specialized tasks changes how much can be produced.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain how the distribution of resources produces international trade and economic interdependence; the causal chain matches the concept but the standard implies rather than states specialization; cross-grade (concept band 3-8).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain the relationship among religious ideas, philosophical ideas, and cultures, i.e. belief systems and their influence on the cultures that hold them.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Names the major world religions through their observances and asks for their significance in the contemporary societies that hold them, satisfying the identify-and-influence test.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to compare how various societies organize the production and distribution of goods and services, i.e. how different systems answer what/how/for whom.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to compare and contrast free enterprise, socialist, and communist economies, which is the concept's 'compare how different economic systems answer those questions' clause.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Same verdict as c-specialization-leads-to-trade, which carries an identical membership test; the two are flagged merge candidates, so an identical description must yield an identical membership.

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to summarize economic and expansionist motivations for European exploration and settlement of lands already inhabited by Indigenous peoples, which is the concept's test verbatim; cross-grade (6-8 concept, TX G4).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks why the Spanish established settlements and Catholic missions, which is the religious-and-political-reasons clause of the concept's test; the Texas locale is context, the motive question is generic. Cross-grade (6-8 concept, TX G4).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to explain why groups explored, colonized, and settled, naming the search for religious freedom and economic gain, which is the concept's test; cross-grade (6-8 concept, TX G5).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to identify the reasons English, Spanish, and French explored and colonized North America, which is the membership test verbatim; cross-grade (concept band 6-8, matches).

links.subject: social_studies
links.rationale: Asks students to compare the political, economic, religious, and social reasons the English colonies were established, i.e. the motives for settling lands already inhabited by Indigenous peoples.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require the student to answer questions about a text by grounding the answer in what the text actually says, though Ohio names the wh- question types and Georgia names the textual reference.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to determine the central message, lesson, or moral of a story; Georgia routes that determination through characters' responses to events, and Georgia has no counterpart to Ohio's retelling clause.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both demand that the student describe how characters respond to major events and challenges in a story.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require describing how sound devices — rhyme, alliteration, repeated lines — work in a poetic or literary text, with nearly identical exemplar lists.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to describe how a story is organized across its whole arc; Ohio frames it as beginning-introduces/ending-concludes and Georgia as the plot events between problem and solution.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require identifying and distinguishing points of view (first vs. third person, narrator vs. character) using evidence of language in the text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Near-verbatim: both require comparing and contrasting two or more versions of the same story by different authors or from diverse cultures.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require answering questions about a text by referring explicitly to specific passages as the basis for the answer.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to determine the central message/theme and explain how details in the text convey it; Georgia narrows the supporting details to character traits and motivations, and does not cover Ohio's retelling clause.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (+1): Georgia introduces the word 'theme' at grade 4, and this element's skill — identify a theme revealed through details about characters — is the closest analogue to Ohio's grade-3 determine-a-theme-through-key-details demand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require describing characters' traits, motivations, and feelings and explaining how those characters function in the story.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers the second half of the Ohio standard: explaining how characters' actions function in and contribute to the plot's sequence of events.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (-1): both require distinguishing literal from nonliteral/figurative language in a text; Georgia places this skill a year earlier than Ohio.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require interpreting the meaning of nonliteral language as used in a text; Georgia names specific devices (similes, metaphors, idioms) rather than Ohio's general literal/nonliteral contrast.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require referring to parts of a text using structural terms such as chapter or section and describing how the arrangement of those parts represents meaning; Ohio adds drama/poetry terms (scene, stanza) that Georgia omits here.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (-1): both require distinguishing first- from third-person narration using language from the text; Georgia places this at grade 2.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require describing the differing points of view of narrators or speakers in a text, though Georgia's emphasis is on separating the narrator's perspective from the reader's own.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require comparing and contrasting characters, settings, central messages, and events across stories; Ohio scopes the comparison to one author's series while Georgia scopes it across cultures.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require answering questions about a text by grounding the answer in specific parts of that text; Ohio names the wh- question types and Georgia names the textual reference.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require identifying the main idea/topic of an expository text and the focus of its individual paragraphs.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Verbatim match: describe the connection between a series of historical events, scientific ideas or concepts, or steps in technical procedures in a text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both rest on knowing what text features (captions, subheadings, indexes) do in a text, but Ohio's demand is using them to locate information efficiently while Georgia's is explaining how they contribute to meaning and organization.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require determining an author's main purpose for a text — what the author is trying to answer, explain, inform, or describe.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require explaining how a graphic element (Ohio: images/diagrams; Georgia: charts and other text features) contributes to and clarifies the meaning of an informational text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require identifying an author's main points/opinion in a persuasive or informational text and explaining how the supporting reasons and facts connect to them.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (+1): both require working across two texts on the same topic in relation to their most important points; Georgia asks the student to integrate and explain where Ohio asks to compare and contrast, and Georgia has no grade-2 two-text element.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require answering questions about a text by referring explicitly to specific passages as the basis for the answer.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require determining the main idea of an expository text and explaining how the facts and key details support it.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Verbatim match: describe the relationship between a series of historical events, scientific ideas or concepts, or steps in technical procedures in a text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both rest on using text features and organizational structures to find and make sense of information, but Ohio's demand is locating relevant information efficiently while Georgia's is describing how the text is arranged.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require the student to distinguish their own perspective from that of the author/narrator of a text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Shared object only in part: Georgia names maps and captions as features that represent information, but its demand is describing how the text is arranged, not Ohio's demand of using illustrations plus words together to understand where, when, why, and how events occur.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require describing the relationship between an author's points/opinion statement and the supporting reasons and evidence used throughout the text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require working across two texts on the same topic in relation to their important points and key details; Georgia asks the student to integrate and explain where Ohio asks to compare and contrast.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require the student to point to specific text details/passages to support an explanation or answer; GA does not name inference, and because GA has no RL/RI split this same evidence-citing element also serves RI.4.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require quoting/referring to specific passages from a text to support an idea or answer; GA adds page-number recording and omits the inference demand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to determine a theme of a literary text from details in the text; GA narrows the details to characters and does not include Ohio's summarizing sub-standard.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to determine a theme and explain how it is conveyed through how a character responds to challenges/conflict; GA lacks only Ohio's summary sub-standard.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to describe a character in depth from text details and how that character functions in the story; GA restricts the analysis to protagonist/antagonist and adds conflict, and omits setting and event.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (GA 3): GA's 'describe characters' traits, motivations, actions, thoughts, and feelings' is the same skill as Ohio's parenthetical (a character's thoughts, words, or actions), though GA additionally ties it to the central message.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require interpreting figurative language (similes, metaphors, idioms) as used in a text; GA asks for the impact of the figurative language rather than Ohio's determination of word meaning.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to explain the structural elements that define poetry as a form; GA covers only the poetry half of Ohio's poem/drama/prose comparison and says nothing about drama's structural elements.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to compare structural/organizational features across texts written in different genres (GA explicitly names rhyme pattern); GA constrains this to texts on the same topic and does not target genre-defining elements like verse, meter, or stage directions.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to explain how the parts of a text (chapters/sections) work together to create the overall structure; GA frames it as features and structure functioning in conjunction rather than Ohio's series-of-parts-to-whole.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to explain/compare the differing points of view and perspectives of narrators or speakers within a text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to describe how a narrator's or speaker's perspective influences the text and how its events are presented.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to explain how non-prose/multimodal choices contribute to a text's overall effect; GA's demand is classifying texts by mode and its effect on audience/purpose, which is narrower than Ohio's analysis of visual elements' contribution to meaning, tone, and mood.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Near-verbatim: both ask the student to compare and contrast the treatment of similar themes and topics and patterns of events in stories, myths, and traditional literature from different cultures.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Near-verbatim: both ask the student to compare and contrast stories in the same genre on their approaches to similar themes and topics.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require the student to point to specific text passages to support an explanation or answer; GA does not name inference, and this element is shared with RL.4.1 because GA has no literature/informational strand split.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require quoting specific passages from a text to support an idea or answer; GA adds page-number recording and omits the inference demand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both center on the main idea of an expository text and the facts and key details that support it; GA's object is the techniques used to present the main idea rather than Ohio's determining it, and GA has no summarizing demand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both address multiple main ideas of an expository text supported by facts and key details; GA's demand is evaluating the presentation techniques rather than Ohio's determining the main ideas and summarizing.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Verbatim match: explain events, procedures, ideas, or concepts in a historical, scientific, or technical text, including what happened and why, based on specific information in the text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Verbatim match: explain the relationships or interactions between two or more individuals, events, ideas, or concepts in a historical, scientific, or technical text based on specific information in the text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (GA 3): both ask the student to describe how a text is organized to present information using organizational structures such as cause/effect and to refer to parts of the text; GA grade 4 replaces this describe-one-text skill with a compare-across-texts one, so the nearest same-skill element sits one grade below.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (GA 5): both ask the student to describe the overall organizational structure (GA names problem/solution) by which a text represents ideas and information; GA couples structure with text features.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (GA 4): both ask the student to compare and contrast the organizational structure of two or more texts; GA constrains the texts to the same topic in different genres.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (GA 5): both ask the student to compare and contrast firsthand and secondhand accounts of the same event or topic; GA has no grade-4 element for this skill.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to compare multiple accounts of the same event or topic and note how they differ; GA fixes the accounts as firsthand/secondhand rather than Ohio's open 'multiple accounts' and does not name perspective.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (GA 6): both ask the student to compare and contrast two authors' accounts/presentations of the same event or topic; GA restricts to historical events and does not name the perspectives represented.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (GA 3): both ask the student to identify non-prose features of visual/auditory modes and explain how they contribute to the text's purpose and effect; GA does not reach Ohio's interpretation of quantitative displays (charts, graphs, timelines), and GA has no closer element at grade 4 or 5.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to locate and draw information from multiple print and digital sources; GA frames this as conducting research on a self-selected topic rather than Ohio's efficient answer-finding/problem-solving.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to explain how an author uses evidence to support the points/claims made in a text; GA restricts the text type to opinion and argumentative texts, while Ohio allows any informational text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to explain how an author's supporting reasons and evidence back the stated claim; GA restricts to opinion/argumentative texts and does not require Ohio's matching of specific evidence to corresponding points.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to integrate information from multiple texts on the same topic in relation to the important points and key details.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Cross-grade (GA 4): both ask the student to integrate information from several texts on the same topic; GA's grade-5 slot in this progression is occupied by firsthand/secondhand accounts, so the integrate-multiple-texts element sits one grade below.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to refer to specific passages or quotations in a text to support an idea or answer; cross-grade (GA 5 to OH 6) because GA's grade 6-8 evidence elements shift to locating evidence across sources with bibliographic recording, and GA never restates the inference half.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Same shared skill of citing specific passages from a text to support an idea or answer; cross-grade (GA 5 to OH 6), and GA frames it inside source curation rather than as a reading-analysis standard, so the inference half is uncovered.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require determining a theme and explaining how the text's particular details convey it, though GA narrows the mechanism to character relationships and omits Ohio's objective-summary requirement.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing how a theme is developed and expressed across a text through characters, events, and plot; GA adds cross-text comparison and has no objective-summary counterpart.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to analyze how a theme is conveyed and developed through its relationship to characters, events/plot, and structure; only Ohio's summary sub-standard is uncovered.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask how narrative technique across the whole text develops plot and characters, matching Ohio's plot-unfolds-in-episodes framing.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to analyze how events, conflict, and characterization drive the plot forward toward resolution.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing how narrative elements/techniques work across a text to develop plot, characters, and setting.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: GA's 'analyze how setting, events, conflict, and characterization influence plot' is nearly Ohio's 'how setting shapes the characters or plot' example; cross-grade (GA 6 to OH 7) because GA moves to narrative devices at grade 7.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask how specific narrative moves across the text reveal character and develop plot, though GA does not single out dialogue or incidents as the unit of analysis.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require determining how figurative and connotative language choices contribute to meaning and tone; GA's 'wide variety of texts' scope covers literature, so no strand crossing.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require explaining how figurative and connotative language choices contribute to meaning, mood, or tone in a text.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Ohio's clause on rhyme and repetitions of sound within a verse or stanza matches GA's analysis of poetic sound devices and stanza structure.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing the impact of figurative, connotative, and literary-device word choices on meaning and tone; GA frames the impact as achieving purpose for an audience.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask how a text's organizational structure conveys meaning, though Ohio's part-to-whole framing (how one scene or stanza fits the overall structure) is narrower than GA's author-modifies-structure framing.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing how an author's structural choices contribute to a text's meaning; GA does not name drama or poem forms specifically.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Ohio's analysis of how a poem's form contributes to meaning matches GA's analysis of poetic techniques including stanza and rhyme scheme.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing how differing organizational structures contribute to meaning and style; GA does not require the two-text comparison Ohio does.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask the student to explain how a narrator's or speaker's perspective is developed and shapes the text; cross-grade (GA 5 to OH 6) because GA's grade 6 element requires two or more competing perspectives, which Ohio does not introduce until grade 7.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing how a single text develops and contrasts two or more perspectives of different narrators or characters; cross-grade (GA 6 to OH 7).

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask how narrative devices produce a calculated effect such as suspense or humor on the audience; GA does not name the point-of-view gap (dramatic irony) as the mechanism, so only the effect-analysis skill is shared.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Near-verbatim: both require comparing and contrasting literary texts in different forms or genres in terms of their approaches to similar themes and topics.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Near-verbatim: both require comparing and contrasting a fictional portrayal of a time, place, or character with a historical account of the same period.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Near-verbatim: both require analyzing how a modern literary work draws on themes, patterns of events, or character types from earlier material.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require identifying the main/central idea of an expository text and the facts and key details that convey it; GA frames this as recognizing expository techniques and has no objective-summary counterpart.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing multiple main ideas and the details that develop them across an expository text; GA has no objective-summary counterpart.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing the development of a central idea over the course of an expository text and its relationship to supporting facts and details; GA has no objective-summary counterpart.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Shared attention to figurative and connotative language in texts, but Ohio's grade 6 standard stops at determining word meaning while GA requires determining impact on meaning, mood, or tone; Ohio's technical-meaning clause has no GA counterpart.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require explaining how a specific figurative or connotative word choice contributes to meaning and tone; GA's 'wide variety of texts' scope covers informational text, so no strand crossing.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing the impact of specific word choices, including figurative and connotative language, on meaning and tone; GA's scope is text-type-neutral.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both ask how organizational structure and features convey meaning and develop ideas; Ohio's part-to-whole framing is narrower than GA's author-modifies-structure framing.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing the structure an author uses to organize a text and how that structure develops the ideas and achieves the author's purpose.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing how structural choices develop and refine a text's ideas; GA does not narrow the unit of analysis to a single paragraph as Ohio does.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require determining an author's purpose in a text and explaining how the text develops it; GA additionally requires handling multiple purposes and audience targeting.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require explaining how an author's perspective is conveyed in the text; GA specifies the author's choice of evidence as the mechanism, which is one of the means Ohio leaves open.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Near-verbatim: both require determining the author's perspective in a text and analyzing how the author distinguishes that position from others.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers the 'purpose' half of Ohio's perspective-or-purpose standard: both require analyzing an author's purpose and how the text develops it for an audience.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers the 'purpose' half of Ohio's standard: both require determining an author's purpose in a text and analyzing how it is developed for specific audiences.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Ohio's demand that students analyze how an author acknowledges and responds to conflicting evidence or viewpoints is the same skill as GA's analysis of an identified counterclaim and rebuttal within an argument.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require using information carried by non-prose modes and text features alongside words to build a coherent understanding of a topic; GA does not frame it as integrating across separate media versions.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require tracing an argument's claim and supporting evidence and judging which claims the evidence actually supports.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing an argument's claim, counterclaim, and conclusion and assessing whether the evidence is relevant and the reasoning logically follows.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require delineating an argument's claim, evidence, counterclaim, and rebuttal and evaluating whether the evidence is relevant, logical, and sufficient.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Near-verbatim: both require comparing and contrasting one author's presentation of events with another author's on the same event or topic.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Verbatim: both require analyzing how two or more authors on the same topic shape their presentations by emphasizing different evidence or advancing different interpretations of facts.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require analyzing and evaluating where two or more texts on the same topic diverge on matters of evidence, fact, or interpretation.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require comparing conflicting perspectives on a single topic across multiple texts and analyzing how each text establishes its position.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers the 'ask' half of Ohio's ask-and-answer questioning routine; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, so it also serves RI.2.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers the 'answer from the text' half — using text evidence to support a response about key details; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.2.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require determining the lesson/theme of a literary work from text evidence; TX literary-elements SE is the direct RL analogue.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Matches Ohio part b, retelling stories while maintaining meaning and logical order; genre-neutral Response strand applied here to literary retelling.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both center on describing the main character, though Ohio frames it as response to events and TX as internal/external traits.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Supplies the 'major events and challenges' side of Ohio's standard via plot's main events, conflict, and resolution, but does not require tying character response to them.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Directly matches Ohio's 'repeated lines' element — explaining the use of repetition; genre-neutral Author's craft strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Rhyme scheme and sound devices in poems is the closest match to Ohio's beats/alliteration/rhyme focus; cross-grade +1 (TX grade 3, no grade-2 sound-device SE exists).

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require describing overall story structure; TX names main events/conflict/resolution where Ohio names beginning and ending.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Identical skill — identifying first- vs third-person narration; genre-neutral Author's craft strand but applied to narrators, so it serves RL.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both involve print/graphic features contributing to meaning, but TX frames it as author's purpose rather than using illustrations to understand character/setting/plot; genre-neutral, shared with RI.2.7.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Matches Ohio's explicit text-to-self and text-to-text connection clause; genre-neutral Comprehension strand (TX has no text-complexity-band standard).

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Describing personal connections to sources covers Ohio's text-to-self/prior-experience clause; genre-neutral Response strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require grounding answers explicitly in text evidence; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.3.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers the question-generation half of Ohio's ask-and-answer standard; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, shared with RI.3.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require inferring/determining a theme of a literary work; TX literary-elements SE is the direct RL analogue.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Matches Ohio part b, retelling stories in ways that maintain meaning and logical order; genre-neutral Response strand applied to literary retelling.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require characterization work at grade 3, though Ohio emphasizes traits/motivations and TX emphasizes major/minor character relationships.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers Ohio's requirement that character actions contribute to the sequence of events, via analyzing plot sequence, conflict, and resolution.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Shared skill is distinguishing literal from figurative/nonliteral language in a text; TX adds an author's-purpose frame Ohio does not require; genre-neutral Author's craft strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Matches Ohio's requirement to use structural terms such as stanza when referring to parts of poems.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers Ohio's drama terms (acts/scenes) but TX asks students to discuss drama elements rather than describe how successive parts build on earlier sections.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Identical skill — identifying first- vs third-person point of view; genre-neutral Author's craft strand applied to narration.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require explaining how graphic/print features contribute to what a text conveys; genre-neutral Author's craft strand, shared with RI.3.7.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Only TX SE in range demanding compare/contrast across texts, but it is genre-neutral and written-response framed, not the literary theme/setting/plot comparison Ohio names; cross-grade +1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Matches Ohio's explicit text-to-self and text-to-text connection clause; genre-neutral Comprehension strand (TX has no text-complexity-band standard).

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Describing personal connections to sources covers Ohio's prior-knowledge/text-to-self clause; genre-neutral Response strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers the 'ask' half of Ohio's ask-and-answer routine; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, same SE also serves RL.2.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers answering from the text with evidence about key details; genre-neutral Response strand, same SE also serves RL.2.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX 2.9D(i) requires recognizing the central idea and supporting evidence of informational text, matching Ohio's main topic / paragraph focus.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Evaluating details to determine key ideas is the same main-idea operation; genre-neutral Comprehension strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX 2.9D(iii) covers informational organizational patterns such as chronological order and explicit cause and effect, matching Ohio's connections among events/ideas/steps; same SE also carries RI.2.2 and RI.2.5 (TX collapses ~all RI structure into one SE).

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX 2.9D(ii) requires using features and graphics to locate and gain information, matching Ohio's use of text features to locate key facts efficiently.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require identifying the author's purpose for writing the text; genre-neutral Author's purpose strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both involve graphic features clarifying a text, but TX frames it as author's purpose rather than explaining how an image contributes to the text; genre-neutral, shared with RL.2.7.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX persuasive-text SE (what the author wants the reader to think/do, fact vs. opinion) is the grade-2 argument analogue to Ohio's author's main points and supporting reasons.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require grounding answers explicitly in text evidence; genre-neutral Response strand, same SE also serves RL.3.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers the question-generation half of Ohio's ask-and-answer standard; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, same SE also serves RL.3.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX 3.9D(i) requires recognizing the central idea with supporting evidence in informational text, matching Ohio's main idea plus supporting key details.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Evaluating details to determine key ideas is the same main-idea-from-details operation; genre-neutral Comprehension strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX 3.9D(iii) covers informational organizational patterns such as cause and effect, matching Ohio's time/sequence/cause-effect relationships among events, ideas, or steps; same SE also carries RI.3.2 and RI.3.5.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX 3.9D(ii) covers informational features (sections, tables, graphs, timelines, bold font) to support understanding, though Ohio additionally requires search tools such as key words and hyperlinks.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both require explaining how graphic/print features contribute to understanding of the text; genre-neutral Author's craft strand, shared with RL.3.7.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX argumentative-text SE (identify the claim, distinguish fact from opinion) is the grade-3 analogue to Ohio's author's points, though it stops short of evidence-to-point relationships.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX 4.9E(ii) 'explaining how the author has used facts for an argument' is the closest match to Ohio's evidence-to-point relationship; cross-grade +1, as grade 3 has no equivalent.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Only TX SE in range demanding compare/contrast of ideas across sources, but it is genre-neutral and written-response framed rather than an informational points/details comparison; cross-grade +1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both demand drawing inferences and citing text evidence to support understanding; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, so it legitimately also anchors RI.4.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Ohio's 'refer to details and examples when explaining' is the same act as using text evidence to support a response; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.4.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both demand inference plus evidence; Ohio's added 'quote accurately' is a precision increase on the same skill; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, shared with RI.5.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Quoting accurately to explain a text is using text evidence to support an appropriate response; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.5.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Ohio 2a (determine a theme from details) and TX 'infer basic themes supported by text evidence' are the same literary-elements skill at the same grade.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers only Ohio 2b (summarize the text); genre-neutral Response strand SE for summarizing, so it also anchors RI.4.2.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Ohio 2a (determine a theme from details) matches TX 'infer multiple themes within a text using text evidence'; TX's plural themes is a modest scope increase on the identical skill.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers only Ohio 2b (summarize the text); genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.5.2.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both analyze characters from textual detail (thoughts, words, actions); TX narrows to interactions and change, so it covers the character half of Ohio's character/setting/event triple.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: TX is the only setting SE and reaches Ohio's 'describe a setting' only through the narrower demand of explaining the setting's influence on plot.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Ohio explicitly cues 'how characters interact' and TX asks students to analyze relationships and conflicts among characters; same comparative character analysis.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Shared object is figurative language (simile, metaphor), but Ohio asks students to determine its meaning while TX asks how it achieves a purpose; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand, and TX's determine-word-meaning SEs are outside this candidate set.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers Ohio's drama half exactly: structural elements of drama including acts, scenes, and stage directions.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Covers Ohio's poem half (structural elements such as stanzas, rhyme, sound devices); cross-grade -1 because TX moves poetry SEs off structure and onto figurative language at grades 4-5.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Shared skill is analyzing text structure, but Ohio asks how parts fit to form a whole while TX asks how structure serves author's purpose; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand used because TX has no literary part-to-whole SE.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both address point of view as a literary device; Ohio adds explaining differences among character perspectives, which TX leaves implicit; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both address narrator point of view (first/third person); Ohio's 'influence how events are described' goes slightly past TX's 'identify and understand'; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both analyze how visual/graphic features contribute to a text's purpose and effect; genre-neutral Author's purpose and craft strand, which is the only TX home for this analysis.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Shared skill is comparing and contrasting ideas across multiple sources; genre-neutral Response strand, since TX has no across-texts theme-comparison SE at grade 4.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Shared skill is comparing and contrasting ideas across a variety of sources; genre-neutral Response strand.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Captures Ohio's theme-across-texts element ('infer multiple themes within and across texts'), but not the same-genre constraint; cross-grade +1, and note TX renumbers literary elements to item 7 at grade 6.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Matches only Ohio's second sentence (activate prior knowledge, make text-to-self and text-to-text connections); genre-neutral Comprehension strand; the text-complexity-band clause has no TX counterpart in this candidate set.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Matches only Ohio's second sentence (prior knowledge, text-to-self and text-to-text connections); genre-neutral Comprehension strand; the text-complexity-band clause is unmatched.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both demand inference plus supporting evidence; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, deliberately shared with RL.4.1 because TX draws no RL/RI distinction here.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Referring to details and examples when explaining a text is using text evidence to support a response; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RL.4.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Both demand inference plus evidence; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, shared with RL.5.1.

links.subject: ela
links.rationale: Quoting accurately to explain a text is using text evidence to support an appropriate response; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RL.5.1.
